branches.name: C-Order — Milan & the West → the Printed Standard

grammars.name: Visconti-Sforza Tarot — The Oldest Tarot (Bonifacio Bembo, c. 1451)
grammars.blurb: The oldest tarot tradition we can hold in our hands. Around 1451 the workshop of Bonifacio Bembo painted a deck of 78 cards in gold leaf and tempera for the ducal house of Visconti-Sforza in Milan — n…

grammars.name: Cary-Yale Visconti Tarot — The Lavish Outlier (c. 1442)
grammars.blurb: The most lavish and most unusual of the surviving hand-painted tarots. Around 1442 the workshop of Bonifacio Bembo painted the Cary-Yale, or Visconti di Modrone, deck in Milan — probably for the marri…

grammars.name: The 'Charles VI' Tarot (Gringonneur) — c. 1450–1480
grammars.blurb: A hand-painted, gold-ground tarot of which 17 cards survive at the Bibliothèque nationale de France — sixteen trumps and a single Page of Swords. It is one of the most beautiful witnesses to 15th-cent…

grammars.name: Minchiate — The 97-Card Florentine Tarot
grammars.blurb: The grandest expansion of tarot ever to see wide use. The Minchiate, played in Florence from the 16th century, swells the deck to 97 cards by adding twenty trumps to the standard sequence: the four cl…

grammars.name: Tarocchino di Bologna — The Real 62-Card Bolognese Deck
grammars.blurb: The genuine Tarocchino di Bologna — the fast 62-card Bolognese game deck (one of Michael Dummett's A-order traditions), built structure-accurate to distinguish it from modern Marseille-named 'Tarocchi…

grammars.name: Tarot de Marseille — Nicolas Conver 1760 (The Printed Standard)
grammars.blurb: The deck the whole occult era treated as canonical. Printed from woodblocks by the master cardmaker Nicolas Conver in Marseille in 1760, this is the most reproduced version of the Tarot de Marseille —…

grammars.name: Tarot de Besançon / Swiss 1JJ — The Juno & Jupiter Variant
grammars.blurb: A regional variant of the Tarot de Marseille (Dummett C-order) defined by one striking change: it replaces the Popess with the goddess JUNO and the Pope with JUPITER, removing both Catholic clergy fro…

grammars.name: Oswald Wirth Tarot — The 22 Esoteric Arcana (1889 / 1926)
grammars.blurb: The hinge of esoteric tarot. In 1889 the Swiss occultist Oswald Wirth (1860–1943), working under Stanislas de Guaita and drawing on Eliphas Levi, designed a deck of 22 Major Arcana — 'Les 22 Arcanes d…

grammars.name: Golden Dawn Tarot — Book T with Rider-Waite-Smith Imagery
grammars.blurb: The Golden Dawn version of the Rider-Waite tarot — Pamela Colman Smith's familiar imagery carried by the full correspondence system of the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn, set down in Book T (Liber …

grammars.name: Court de Gébelin's Tarot — The Birth of the 'Book of Thoth' (1781)
grammars.blurb: The 22 trump plates from Antoine Court de Gébelin's 'Le Monde Primitif' (vol. VIII, 1781) — the essay that INVENTED esoteric tarot. Looking at an ordinary Tarot de Marseille, Court de Gébelin declared…

grammars.name: Etteilla I
grammars.blurb: The original deck — Jean-Baptiste Alliette's 'Livre de Thot', the first tarot ever designed specifically for divination, with Basan's engravings and the Egyptian/Hermetic framing Etteilla invented.

grammars.name: Etteilla II
grammars.blurb: The second edition, issued by Simon Blocquel (under the name 'Julia Orsini'), with redesigned letterpress panels — the form in which the Etteilla system spread most widely.

grammars.name: Grand Jeu de l'Oracle des Dames (Etteilla III)
grammars.blurb: The third edition — G. Regamey's vibrant chromolithographs for the 'Grand Jeu de l'Oracle des Dames', the most decorative of the three Etteilla editions.

grammars.name: The 'Mantegna Tarocchi' — A Ladder of Being (NOT a Tarot), c. 1465
grammars.blurb: Fifty engravings from c. 1465 that are constantly mistaken for a tarot — and are not one. The 'Mantegna Tarocchi' (not by Mantegna, and not a tarot) is a Neoplatonic LADDER of being: five decades of t…

grammars.name: The Tree of Tarot — A Grammar of Tarot Grammars
grammars.blurb: The genealogy of tarot as a single explorable tree: the decks ARE the cards. Built to render in the recursive.eco tree-viewer, with L1 = individual decks/traditions, L2 = the branches (Michael Dummett…

grammars.name: People & Institutions of Tarot — The Hands Behind the Cards
grammars.blurb: The makers, patrons, occultists, scholars, and institutions behind the historical tarot decks in this collection. Each node links to the deck(s) that person or body made, reframed, printed, or holds —…

grammars.name: Ma Diao (馬吊) — Chinese Money-Suited Cards (the Deep Root)
grammars.blurb: The deepest root of the playing-card form: a money-suited Chinese game (Ming-era), shown via the 12 public-domain Skokloster Castle survivors. A deep root, not a direct ancestor of tarot.

grammars.name: Jacques Viéville Tarot — Paris, c. 1650 (BnF)
grammars.blurb: One of the three oldest surviving Tarots de Marseille (Paris, c.1650) — the great outlier, leaning toward the Eastern/Belgian branch. 78 woodcut cards from the BnF/Gallica public-domain scan.

grammars.name: Belgian Tarot — Vandenborre / 'Tarot Flamand' (Brussels, c. 1780)
grammars.blurb: The Flemish tarot (Vandenborre, Brussels c.1780): the Marseille trumps with two famous swaps — the Captain for the Popess and Bacchus for the Pope. 22 Major Arcana, public domain.

grammars.name: Anonymous Parisian Tarot — BnF (Tarot de Marseille, 17th–18th c.)
grammars.blurb: The third of the three early Parisian tarots (BnF, 17th–18th c.) — a fully named Tarot de Marseille, companion to Noblet and Viéville. 78 woodcut cards from the BnF/Gallica public-domain scan.

grammars.name: d'Este Tarocchi — The Ferrarese Fragment (c. 1450)
grammars.blurb: Among the oldest tarot of all — 16 surviving hand-painted Ferrarese cards (c.1450, House of Este), the B-order home. High-res open-access scans from Yale Beinecke / Cary Collection.

grammars.name: Mamluk Playing Cards — Mulūk wa-nuwwāb (the Direct Ancestor)
grammars.blurb: The single most important upstream object in this repo: the four-suit pack Europe copied. The Mamluk kanjifah (Egypt & Syria, c. 1500) — four suits of ten pips plus three courts (aniconic) — is the direct structural ancestor of European playing cards and thus of tarot itself.

grammars.name: Ganjifa — Persian & Mughal Cards (the Cousin, not the Parent)
grammars.blurb: The Persian and Mughal circular card tradition — a cousin branch that shares deep Islamic roots with the Mamluk pack but did not become the ancestor of European cards or tarot.

grammars.name: Sola Busca Tarot — The First Fully-Engraved Deck (N. Italy, c. 1491)
grammars.blurb: The first tarot deck engraved with fully scenic pip cards (Northern Italy, c. 1491) — a unique alchemical-mythological programme. The deck that directly inspired Pamela Colman Smith's pip designs for the Rider-Waite-Smith.

grammars.name: Jean Noblet Tarot — The Oldest Surviving Complete Tarot de Marseille Deck (Paris, c. 1650)
grammars.blurb: The oldest surviving complete Tarot de Marseille deck (Paris, c. 1650, Jean Noblet) — a companion to the Viéville and Anonymous Parisian tarots, all from the BnF/Gallica public-domain scans.

grammars.name: The Cary Sheet — Earliest Marseille Pattern (Milan, c. 1500)
grammars.blurb: An uncut sheet of tarot cards (Milan, c. 1500) representing the earliest known example of the Marseille pattern — a bridge between the hand-painted Visconti tradition and the printed Marseille standard.

grammars.name: The Rosenwald Sheet — Early Florentine (A-order), c. 1500
grammars.blurb: An uncut Florentine tarot sheet (c. 1500) following the A-order trump sequence — held at the National Gallery of Art (Rosenwald Collection). One of the rarest early tarot documents.
